setup: Your AI coding assistant has a 12% hallucination rate on TypeScript questions (verified by your QA team). Your largest enterprise customer's annual review is in 2 weeks. What do you ship first?

choices.label: Ship a 'verify before using' warning banner above every answer
Tune the system prompt to add 'be conservative when uncertain'
Add RAG over the official TypeScript docs as grounding
Build an eval pipeline and a rollback gate before changing anything else

reasoning: Without an eval you can't know if A, B, or C moved the needle — you'll be flying blind in front of the customer. D is the foundation: define ground truth, score current behaviour, then iterate on B/C with measurement. A is a band-aid that erodes trust; B is unmeasurable; C helps but you can't prove it without D.

setup: Your LLM-powered support chat has 4.2 s p95 latency. Drop-off after the first message is 38%, vs 11% for the legacy keyword-based bot. Engineering says GPT-4 is the floor on quality.

choices.label: Stream tokens to the UI so users see characters as they generate
Switch to a smaller model (Haiku / GPT-4o-mini) for the first response
Cut the system prompt from 4k tokens to 800 by trimming examples

reasoning: Streaming is the cheapest, lowest-risk perceived-latency win — users see content in under 500 ms and stay engaged even if total response takes 5 s. B/C/D are real improvements but each carries quality risk and shipping cost. A doesn't change the model or prompt; it changes the UX. Ship A this sprint, evaluate B/C/D with an eval pipeline.

setup: Engineering proposes swapping the primary LLM from GPT-4 to Claude Sonnet to cut inference cost by 38%. Their benchmark on 200 internal prompts shows quality is 'roughly the same'.

choices.label: Approve — the cost savings are material and 200 prompts is a decent sample
Reject — you can't risk a model swap on a benchmark of 200
Ship a percentage rollout (5 → 25 → 100) gated on production-eval scores + user-feedback drop
Run both models in shadow for a week and compare on the production traffic distribution

reasoning: A is reckless — 200 prompts can't capture the long tail of real production behaviour. B over-indexes on caution and leaves the cost win on the table. D is good for measurement but takes a week of extra compute and doesn't actually ship. C does both: real production data, gated rollout, automatic rollback. Most senior PM answer.

setup: A user posts a screenshot on Twitter showing your AI cooking-assistant told them to 'use bleach to deglaze the pan'. 200k+ likes in 6 hours. Your CEO is texting. What's your move in the next 60 minutes?

choices.label: Roll back the model to the previous version immediately
Add a hardcoded filter blocking 'bleach' as a system-prompt rule, ship in 10 minutes
Public statement, then form a working group to study the issue
Investigate: pull the trace, confirm the screenshot is real, identify whether it's a prompt-injection or model failure, then decide between rollback and a targeted fix

reasoning: Speed is real but you cannot fix what you don't understand. The first 60 minutes is for evidence, not action. The screenshot might be doctored, might be prompt-injected, might be a known edge case — each implies a different response. A is correct ONLY if D's investigation confirms a model regression. B is fast but reactive; you'll be playing whack-a-mole. C delays without acting. D is the senior-PM move: triage with evidence, then act.
